county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1868 from Dona Ana County. Named for Ulysses S. Grant. Silver City is county seat — major silver and copper mining center. Luna, Hidalgo, and parts of Sierra and Catron carved from Grant.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grant County is the heart of New Mexico's mining heritage. Silver City boomed in the 1870s with silver strikes, and the Santa Rita copper mine (operational since 1800 under Spanish rule) is one of the oldest mines in North America. Mining company employment records, mine inspection reports, and union records trace thousands of families, including many Mexican and Chinese immigrant miners. Billy the Kid spent part of his youth in Silver City. County Clerk has deeds from 1868. Western NM University in Silver City houses the Miller Library Special Collections with mining and ranching manuscripts. The Silver City Museum and Grant County Historical Society hold local family files. Catholic parish records from San Vicente de Paul Church date to the 1870s. Apache conflict records (Geronimo, Victorio) at NARA document frontier military families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person at County Clerk, Silver City

source_profiles.source_name: Grant County Deed & Mining Claim Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books and mining claim records from 1868. Extensive mining claim filings for Silver City, Pinos Altos, Mogollon districts.
Marriage licenses from territorial period.
Wills, estates. Mining-era estates with mineral rights transfers.
District court records including mining disputes, criminal cases, and naturalization proceedings.
Federal census 1870-1950. Mining camp enumerations include diverse ethnic populations.

source_profiles.source_name: Silver City Enterprise & Grant County Herald
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mining-era newspapers with legal notices, mining claim filings, obituaries.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1891 from San Miguel and Lincoln counties. Named for Our Lady of Guadalupe. Santa Rosa is county seat — on the Pecos River and Route 66.
county_metadata.research_tips: Guadalupe County lies along the Pecos River in east-central NM, with a predominantly Hispanic population tracing ancestry to Spanish colonial settlers who moved east from the Rio Grande Valley. County Clerk in Santa Rosa has deeds from 1891; pre-1891 records are in San Miguel (Las Vegas) and Lincoln (Carrizozo) counties. Catholic parish records from Santa Rosa and Puerto de Luna parishes are on FamilySearch and document extended Hispanic families. Route 66 ran through Santa Rosa from 1926; businesses and traveler records reflect mid-century culture. De Baca County was carved from Guadalupe in 1917. SRCA in Santa Fe holds territorial court records for the county. Sheep ranching was the primary economy — brand records and livestock association files can trace ranching families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person at County Clerk, Santa Rosa

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation.

source_profiles.source_name: Santa Rosa & Puerto de Luna Parish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Guadalupe County communities. Key for Hispanic families.
Marriage licenses from 1891.
Federal census 1900-1950. Pre-1891 area under San Miguel County.
Santa Rosa, Puerto de Luna, and rural community cemeteries.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1921 from Mora and Union counties. Named for Warren G. Harding. Least populated county in NM. Mosquero is county seat.
county_metadata.research_tips: Harding County is New Mexico's least populated county with fewer than 700 residents, making it one of the smallest jurisdictions in the US. Pre-1921 records are in Mora (Mora) and Union (Clayton) counties. County Clerk in Mosquero has limited records from 1921 forward. Ranching and homesteading families dominate; brand records and BLM homestead patents are key sources. The county's isolation means church records (Catholic parishes in Roy and Mosquero) are especially valuable for family connections. NMGenWeb volunteers have transcribed some cemetery records. SRCA in Santa Fe holds territorial-era records for the parent counties.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Pre-1921 records in Mora and Union counties.
Marriage licenses from 1921.
Wills and estates from formation. Very limited volume.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM GLO — Harding County Homestead Patents
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal homestead patents for NE NM plains.
Small rural cemeteries in Mosquero, Roy, and surrounding ranching communities.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Grant County. Named for the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo (1848). Lordsburg is county seat — railroad town on the Southern Pacific. Boot Heel region borders Mexico and Arizona.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hidalgo County in NM's southwestern Boot Heel region borders both Mexico and Arizona, creating complex jurisdictional research patterns. Pre-1919 records are in Grant County (Silver City). County Clerk in Lordsburg has deeds from 1919. Lordsburg was a railroad division point on the Southern Pacific; railroad employment records at UP/SP archives trace many families. The Boot Heel area saw significant ranching, border conflict, and Pancho Villa-era activity. Cross-border families require Mexican civil and church records from Chihuahua. Mining records from the Lordsburg and Shakespeare mining districts supplement land records. The Hidalgo County Museum has local collections. Check NARA for border patrol and customs records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Pre-1919 in Grant County.
Marriage licenses from 1919.
Wills and estates from formation.

source_profiles.source_name: Border Crossing Records — Lordsburg & Columbus Districts
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mexican border crossing records for SW NM ports of entry.
Federal census from 1920 (first after formation).

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from Chaves and Eddy counties. Named for Joseph C. Lea, Roswell pioneer. Lovington is county seat. Hobbs is largest city — major oil and gas center (Permian Basin).
county_metadata.research_tips: Lea County is the center of New Mexico's oil and gas industry in the Permian Basin, and the oil boom of the 1920s-1930s transformed the region. Pre-1917 records are in Chaves (Roswell) and Eddy (Carlsbad) counties. County Clerk in Lovington has deeds from 1917. Hobbs, the county's largest city, grew rapidly with oil discovery in 1927 — oil company employment records and lease files trace thousands of workers who migrated from Texas and Oklahoma. The Lea County Museum in Lovington has local history materials. Ranching predated oil; brand records at the NM Livestock Board trace early families. Protestant churches dominate — check Baptist and Church of Christ records. The Western Heritage Museum in Hobbs has oil industry genealogical resources.

source_profiles.source_name: Lea County Deed & Oil Lease Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books and oil/gas lease records from 1917. Oil lease filings dominate after 1927.
Marriage licenses from 1917.
Wills and estates. Oil royalty interests feature in many estates.
Federal census from 1920. Population surged after oil discovery.

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Lea County Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Lovington, Hobbs, Eunice, Jal cemeteries.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known catastrophic losses; Lincoln County War-era records well preserved.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1869 from Socorro and Dona Ana counties. Originally vast — covered most of SE NM. Chaves, Eddy, Otero, and Roosevelt carved out. Lincoln was original county seat until 1913 when moved to Carrizozo. Famous for the Lincoln County War (1878-1881) and Billy the Kid.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County is famous for the Lincoln County War (1878-1881), which produced extensive court records now held at SRCA in Santa Fe and at the Lincoln Historic Site. Billy the Kid's trial and escape records are among the most researched documents in NM history. The county originally covered much of SE NM before daughter counties were carved out. County Clerk in Carrizozo has deeds from 1869; earlier records were kept at the old county seat of Lincoln. Lincoln State Monument preserves the historic courthouse. The Mescalero Apache Reservation is within county boundaries — tribal and BIA records at NARA are essential for Apache families. Ranching dominated the economy; check NM Livestock Board brand records. The Ruidoso area developed later as a resort community. Fort Stanton records (1855-1953) at NARA document military and Civilian Conservation Corps families.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1869. Ranching and cattle company land transfers dominant.

source_profiles.repository: SRCA, Santa Fe / Lincoln Historic Site
source_profiles.coverage_notes: District court records including Lincoln County War cases, Billy the Kid trial records, and territorial-era criminal/civil cases.
Marriage licenses from territorial period.
Census rolls, allotment records, agency correspondence for Mescalero Apache Reservation.
Military post returns, personnel records, and Civilian Conservation Corps records from Fort Stanton.
Federal census 1870-1950. Originally vast county territory.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known losses; county created from federal land.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1949 from Sandoval and Santa Fe counties. Site of Manhattan Project and Los Alamos National Laboratory. Unique NM county — smallest by area, created around a federal installation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Los Alamos County is unique in NM — created in 1949 around the Manhattan Project site. Pre-WWII, the area was the Los Alamos Ranch School and surrounding homesteads in Sandoval and Santa Fe counties. Wartime (1943-1945) records are classified or restricted; the Los Alamos Historical Society and Museum have unclassified materials. County Clerk has records from 1949. Many early residents came as scientists and support staff from across the US — check home-state records for their origins. The Bradbury Science Museum has institutional history. The Atomic Heritage Foundation maintains oral histories. For pre-1943 families, check Sandoval County (Bernalillo) and Santa Fe County records. San Ildefonso Pueblo borders the county — Pueblo records separate.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person at County Clerk, Los Alamos

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Most land was originally federal; private land sales began post-1949.
Marriage licenses from 1949.
Wills and estates from formation.
First federal census as separate county in 1950. Pre-1943 area under Sandoval/Santa Fe.

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Los Alamos Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Guaje Pines Cemetery and other Los Alamos burial sites.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1901 from Grant and Dona Ana counties. Named for Solomon Luna, NM political leader. Deming is county seat. Columbus, NM — site of Pancho Villa's 1916 raid — is in Luna County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Luna County includes Columbus, NM, where Pancho Villa's 1916 raid triggered the Punitive Expedition — military records at NARA (RG 94, RG 395) are extensive and document both military families and border residents. Pre-1901 records are in Grant (Silver City) and Dona Ana (Las Cruces) counties. County Clerk in Deming has deeds from 1901. The Deming-Luna Mimbres Museum has local history and genealogy materials. The county's proximity to Mexico makes cross-border research important — check border crossing records and Mexican civil registries in Chihuahua. Camp Furlong/Columbus military post records at NARA. Southern Pacific Railroad employment records trace many families who settled along the rail line. Deming headlight newspaper is partially digitized.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Pre-1901 in Grant and Dona Ana counties.
Marriage licenses from 1901.
Military records from Columbus garrison and the Punitive Expedition against Pancho Villa (1916-1917). Post returns, personnel rosters, casualty reports.

source_profiles.source_name: Columbus Port of Entry — Border Crossing Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Border crossing records for Columbus, NM port of entry.
Federal census from 1910.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1899 from Bernalillo and Valencia counties. Named for President William McKinley. Gallup is county seat — railroad town. Navajo Nation and Zuni Pueblo occupy large portions. Cibola County carved out 1981.
county_metadata.research_tips: McKinley County has the largest Native American population percentage of any NM county, with the Navajo Nation covering the northern portion and Zuni Pueblo in the south. Gallup, the county seat, developed as a railroad and trading town. County Clerk in Gallup has deeds from 1899. For Navajo families, the Navajo Nation Vital Records office in Window Rock, AZ, and BIA records at NARA Denver are essential. Zuni Pueblo maintains its own records; BIA Southern Pueblos Agency records at NARA cover Zuni. Trading post records (Hubbell, Kirk Brothers) document economic relationships between Navajo/Zuni and traders. Catholic and Christian Reformed mission records serve Native communities. Coal mining records from the Gallup area trace Italian, Slavic, and Mexican immigrant miners. The Gallup Cultural Center has regional materials.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Much land is tribal trust land not in county deed system.

source_profiles.source_name: Navajo Nation & Zuni Pueblo BIA Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Navajo Agency / BIA Southern Pueblos
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Census rolls, allotment records, agency correspondence, and school records for Navajo Nation and Zuni Pueblo within McKinley County.
source_profiles.survival_notes: At NARA Denver and Window Rock

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from 1899. Navajo traditional marriages not in county records.

source_profiles.repository: Catholic Diocese / Christian Reformed Church / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic mission records at Sacred Heart (Gallup) and Zuni mission. Christian Reformed mission records for Navajo and Zuni communities.
Federal census 1900-1950. Separate Indian schedules for Navajo and Zuni enumeration.
Coal mining era newspapers covering labor disputes, Native American affairs, railroad news.
